sections.name: Hook — Mirroring the Skepticism
sections.content:
You've tried everything.

I know that's not hyperbole. When L2 parents say "everything," they mean it: BCBA protocols, OT sensory work, social stories, sticker charts, timer sits, potty watches, the 3-day naked method, Oh Crap, rewards systems, peer motivation, Daniel Tiger episodes, scheduled sits every 30 minutes, plastic on the couch, garbage bags on cushions, going back to naked, rewards (candy, gummies), constant prompting, withholding drinks before bed, waking them every 2 hours at night. You've had conversations with your BCBA that lasted longer than most parents' entire potty training journey.

You've spent thousands on therapy. Read the books. Downloaded the charts. Bought the special potties. Joined the Facebook groups for support. Posted at 2am asking if anyone, anyone, has been through this.

And here you are. The same place you started. Maybe further back, because hope has taken more hits.

---

### Objection Naming — Naming the Real Truth

"I've tried everything. 40+ methods. $50,000 in therapy. Nothing worked."

That's not exaggeration, that's trauma. Method cycling is its own kind of exhaustion. Every new approach comes with a tiny spark of hope, and every failure extinguishes it a little more. You've learned to be skeptical because skepticism has been correct, over and over.

You're not wrong to be skeptical. Skepticism is earned. You've earned it.

---

### Reframe — The Wrong Battle

Here's what I need you to understand: every single one of those methods assumed your child CAN feel the signal.

Oh Crap assumes it. Sticker charts assume it. Scheduled sits assume it. Every BCBA protocol that uses timer-based prompting assumes the internal "I need to go" sensation arrives on time, and that the issue is compliance, awareness, or motivation.

For 40+ methods, you've been fighting the wrong battle.

The research is settled: many autistic children have diminished body awareness (Nicholson et al. 2019, Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders). The brain doesn't receive body signals, bladder fullness, temperature, hunger, the same way neurotypical brains do. The "I need to go" message arrives. It just arrives too late.

That's not a training problem. That's a neurological signal problem. No method that assumes the signal works correctly can fix a broken signal.

---

sections.name: Mechanism — The Science of Body-Signal Learning
sections.content:
This where the body-signal underwear is fundamentally different from every method you've tried.

Interoception — the sense that tells us when we're hungry, thirsty, or need the bathroom — for many autistic children, that signal doesn't arrive on time. The "I need to go" message travels from bladder to brain, but arrives milliseconds to minutes too late. That's not defiance. That's a neurological timing gap.

Interoception CAN be trained. Research showed an 8-week targeted intervention produced measurable improvement in body awareness. The body-signal underwear creates the conditions for that training in everyday life.

Layer 1, Signal Creation: A gentle, sustained wetness sensation lasting 30-60 seconds. Most training underwear wicks moisture away instantly, removing the feedback the brain needs. The BSL inner layer holds moisture long enough for the brain to register: *something happened.*

Layer 2, Smart Protection: After those critical seconds, the absorption layer activates — protects furniture and clothing without wicking the learning signal away entirely.

Layer 3, Leak Resistance: The outer barrier contains accidents without creating "diaper mode."

The outer barrier contains accidents without creating "diaper mode." Your child feels the consequence of the full bladder release, feels it enough to learn from it, without the humiliation of a soaked couch or the disruption of a full clothing change every time.

Three layers. One purpose: bridge the gap between "the signal should be here" and "the signal actually arrives."

---

sections.name: Social Proof — Real L2 Parents
sections.content:
> "He will sit on the toilet for 45 minutes and then pee the second he stands up."
>, Real L2 parent, before Body-Signal

> "After 2 weeks in BKC undies, he paused. Then he looked down. Then he walked to the potty. Small, but that never happened before."
>, Real L2 parent, 4 weeks in

> "If I could just get him the signal, I know the rest would follow."
>, Real L2 parent, describing exactly what BKC provides

Your child isn't broken. The methods weren't built for your child's nervous system.

---

sections.name: Guarantee — The Only Promise We Make
sections.content:
I can't promise your child will potty train. No one can. Every child's nervous system is different, and biology doesn't follow marketing timelines.

What I can promise: 60 days to try, by your judgment. If your child shows any sign of body awareness, a pause, a look, a step toward the potty, anything that wasn't there before, you'll know the signal is working. If nothing changes, full refund. Not because it didn't work. Because your judgment matters, and you're the only expert on your child.

---

sections.name: CTA
sections.content:
[ 60 Days. Zero Risk. Your Judgment. ]

When you're ready to try.
